body.text: Texas felt different the moment I crossed the state line.
The energy shifted and I knew I made the right decision. For me. For my kid.
I got into fitness and started living at the gym. I got in the best shape of my life   — and met lots of new friends that fueled me.
Those friends helped me clear the wreckage and start to rebuild.
I immersed myself into personal development — 80+ books in my first year alone.
I started attending personal development conferences and seminars — and met even more friends who were building, growing, and reaching.
As my circle grew, so did the opportunities. High performance coaching. Business consulting. Fun and friends around every corner.
Life was good. I was able to be the mom I wanted to be — and that was always the point.
I still struggled. The turbulence beneath the surface — depression, anxiety, trauma — still played a prominent role.
It wasn't a straight ascent. But the altitude was increasing.
Thing was — underneath all of it, I still didn't feel complete. My story didn't feel complete.
I would find people that accepted me into their circle — for a time — but each time the circle drifted, I lost who I was again.
The darkness would creep back in.
Nothing and no one made me feel quite like I did in my first years in the military.
And quietly I carried shame and regret about leaving when I did — knowing my story never felt quite complete.

credentials.label: Years Without a True Sense of Self
